{"step_by_step_thinking": "The question asks about the presence of mast cells in normal and inflamed pulp. From the MEDCORP source document (Document [0]), it is explicitly stated that the pulp mass contains various cell types, including fibroblasts, odontoblasts, histiocytes, macrophages, mast cells, and plasma cells. This indicates that mast cells are present in the normal pulp. The same document does not state that mast cells are absent in inflamed pulp; rather, it lists mast cells as one of the cell types present in the pulp tissue. While some documents mention mast cells in other contexts (e.g., in inflammation or granulomas), none indicate that mast cells are absent in inflamed pulp. In fact, mast cells are known to be involved in inflammatory responses, and their presence in inflamed tissues is typical. Therefore, since mast cells are present in normal pulp and are likely involved in the inflammatory response, they are also present in inflamed pulp. Thus, the correct answer is that mast cells are present in normal pulp, and there is no evidence they are absent in inflamed pulp. Option B is supported by the evidence.", "answer_choice": "B"}
